mnemonics.title: Cloud Migration Strategies
mnemonics.content: RRRR - Rehost (Lift & Shift), Replatform, Refactor (Re-architect), Retain (Don't migrate)

mnemonics.title: AWS Well-Architected Pillars
mnemonics.content: ROSE - Reliability, Operational Excellence, Security, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability

mnemonics.title: Cloud Benefits
mnemonics.content: SCALE - Scalability, Cost-effectiveness, Agility, Elasticity, Global Access

acronyms.usage: Virtual servers and storage (AWS EC2, Azure VMs)
Complete cost analysis including CAPEX and OPEX

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a city with specialized buildings (services) - each building handles one function (restaurants, hospitals, shops) connected by roads (APIs), allowing independent expansion of any service without affecting others.
Think of a restaurant that automatically opens more tables (instances) during dinner rush and closes them during slow periods, without manual intervention.
Imagine a stadium security checkpoint - multiple gates opening based on crowd density to prevent bottlenecks at any single entrance.
Picture a constellation where each star (Availability Zone) is close to others in the same constellation (Region) but isolated enough to survive if one star fails.
Like hiring a taxi only when you need to travel - you don't own the car (infrastructure) but get the service when needed, paying only for the trip (execution time).

analogies.analogy: Vertical scaling is like replacing a small car with a bigger bus to carry more passengers (more power). Horizontal scaling is like adding more small cars to a fleet (more capacity through distribution).
Like a small bakery that normally handles local customers but hires additional help and rents extra kitchen space during holiday seasons to handle surge orders.
Like using different banks for different services - one for savings, another for investments, a third for credit cards - to get best rates and avoid dependency on one institution.
Like how shopping malls attract more shops because customers already visit - data attracts applications and analytics tools to where the data resides.
Like booking flight tickets at the last minute for cheap prices - you get a discount but risk the flight being cancelled if seats fill up.
